questions.question: A fibre-optic link is physically cut. The local router's interface still shows 'up'. Which OSPF timer controls how long before the neighbor is declared dead?
options: The SPF throttle delay
The Hello interval
The Dead interval
The LSA refresh timer
questions.explanation: When there is no carrier-loss signal (the local interface stays up), OSPF cannot detect the failure directly. It must wait for the Dead interval — the time after the last received Hello — before declaring the neighbor down. The default is 40 seconds.

questions.question: What is the default relationship between OSPF's Hello interval and Dead interval?
options: Dead = Hello / 2
Dead = Hello × 4
Dead = Hello + 30
They are independent and unrelated
questions.explanation: By convention (and RFC 2328 recommendation), the Dead interval is 4× the Hello interval. With the default 10-second Hello, the Dead interval is 40 seconds. Both ends of a link must agree on these values or they will not form an adjacency.

questions.question: You take down veth12 in R1's namespace (ip link set veth12 down). How does OSPF on R1 detect the failure?
options: It waits for the Dead interval to expire
It detects the interface-down kernel event immediately
It waits for the next Hello to time out
It runs a traceroute to R2 every second
questions.explanation: When an interface loses carrier, the kernel generates an interface-down netlink event. ospfd (via zebra) receives this event immediately and reacts: it marks the link down, originates an updated Router LSA, and floods it. No timer wait is needed for carrier-loss failures.

questions.question: What does 'ospf timers throttle spf 0 10 1000' mean?
options: Run SPF every 10 milliseconds, up to 1000 times per second
Wait 0ms after a trigger, then hold 10ms before the next run, doubling up to 1000ms max
Delay all LSA flooding by 10ms to reduce CPU load
Set the Dead interval to 0ms and Hello to 1000ms
questions.explanation: The three values are: initial delay before the first SPF run (0ms), initial hold between runs (10ms), and max hold (1000ms). If failures keep arriving, the hold doubles each time (10 → 20 → 40 → ... → 1000ms) to prevent CPU thrashing during topology storms.

questions.question: You measure a 38-second outage when R2's ospfd process is killed (interface stays up). What single configuration change would most reduce this outage?
options: Increase the SPF throttle max-hold to 10000ms
Add a second physical link between R1 and R2
Reduce the Dead interval from 40s to 4s on all interfaces
Increase the Hello interval from 10s to 20s
questions.explanation: A 38-second outage is almost entirely the Dead interval. Reducing Dead interval to 4s (Hello=1s, Dead=4s) would cut the outage to ~4 seconds. Adding a second link helps resilience but does not change detection time. Increasing Hello or SPF hold makes things worse.
